segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]

MEGHAN MEHTA: Hi, I'm
Meghan, a developer relations
engineer on the Android team.

I'm joined by Halil, a software
engineer on the Compose team,

and I'm also joined by
Bubba, a Goldendoodle.
[BALL SQUEAKS]

Today, we're going to talk about
how you can use Compose Text

APIs to create functional,
exceptional, and delightful user
input experiences.

We'll be using a pet social
media app to showcase these APIs

and to give Bubba a place
for his barks to be heard.

We'll be starting with an app
that looks great but isn't

properly using the robust
Compose Text APIs to make
it feel as great as it looks.

Before we get to the
app, I want to introduce
the new state-based TextField.

This is our new best
practice for TextField usage,

and you'll see it used in so
many ways throughout this talk.

Here are some of the features
of the new TextFields.

We've switched to a different
state hoisting paradigm.

By replacing value
and callback hoisting,

the TextField state provides
a more complete and reliable

approach to managing
the state of a TextField

We've replaced the
on-value change callback

with the new InputTransformation
mechanism for handling
input modifications.

We've updated the
VisualTransformation

to OutputTransformation for
managing how text is presented.

And we've tidied up single
and multiline configuration
parameters.
OK, back to the dogs.

We'll start with the
registration screen.

Right now, the
registration screen

takes in a username
and a password.

It works and allows
a pup to register,

but everyone around him
can see his password
when he's typing it in.

And after registering, he may
be thinking about squirrels

and forget about the
credentials he just chose.

In this section, we'll
update the password field

to hide text as our puppy is
typing it in for security, add

a password requirement
for extra security,

and we'll save our pup's
super secure credentials
to their password manager.

Here's our username
field that utilizes
the new state-based TextField.

We can see the standard
state defined in line,

the line limit to a single
line, and the placeholder text
of "Username."

For the password field,
we'll be using the brand
new SecureTextField.

Key features are
hiding password text

by default and disabling
cut and copy actions.

The implementation is similar
to that of the TextField.
We'll set the state.

In this case, you can see it's
been hoisted and then passed in,

and the placeholder
will be set as password.

Unlike in TextField, we don't
have to specify the line limit.

SecureTextFields are
always single line.

Just by switching
to SecureTextField,

we gave our puppies a
safer user experience.

Another important
safety aspect is

to add password requirements
to make sure our pup chooses
a secure password.

We'll disable the Sign Up
button unless the password input
is at least eight characters.

We'll use our hoisted state to
enable the Sign Up button based
on the password length.

It works, and the
button is only enabled
when the requirement is met.

However, the puppy doesn't
know what the requirements
are and may be confused.

We can add two things to
make the requirements clearer
to the pup.

The supportingText
parameter allows

us to add text defining
what the requirement is.

And the isError parameter
will put the TextField

in an error state when
the length of the password

is between one and
seven characters.

This allows us to enforce
our password requirements

while also communicating
them clearly to our puppies.

Let's add code to
save their credentials

so they don't forget their
new super secure password.

We'll be saving credentials
using the new Compose Autofill
APIs.

While testing this
feature, you'll

need to opt in to Autofill
and enable a password

manager in the Settings
app of your device.

In order to make your
TextField Autofill enabled,

add a semantics modifier to
your TextField's modifier.

And inside, add a
contentType parameter.

ContentType is the way that we
specify to the system what kind
of content to save or fill.

There are many
Autofill ContentTypes
for many different use cases.

For example, if you were
using a PhoneNumber TextField,

the PhoneNumber ContentType
would be useful to add

and would indicate to the system
that you want to fill a phone
number if there is one saved.

For the registration
page, we'll be

using NewUsername and
NewPassword, which allows a pup

to save their
username and password

credentials to their password
manager when they register.

We will set the ContentType
of the username TextField

to be NewUsername and then
set the SecureTextField's type
to NewPassword.

We have the information, but
need to commit the information
to the password manager.

To do this, add a value
to hold a reference
to an Autofill manager.

Then we can call
autofillManager.commit
when the button is clicked.

When you press Sign Up,
you'll see a bottom sheet

with a prompt to save
credentials to the password
manager.

Now that our pup has
registered for an account,
let's use it to log in.

Currently, this
screen has TextFields

for the doggy to input
their username and password.

The problem is, our puppy
did chase a squirrel right

after going through registration
and doesn't remember
his credentials at all.

And while he appreciates
the password field

being more secure,
he wants to be

able to toggle the
password's visibility.

In this section, we will
update the functionality

by filling in the pup's
credentials for them,

and we'll update the button in
the password TextField to toggle
the password visibility.

Let's take these TextFields
in the login screen
and make them autofill enabled.

This time, we will use
username and password

for the ContentTypes Using these
ContentTypes tells the system

to see if there are any
username-password combos

in the password manager
that can be filled.

When the username
field is clicked into,

the system will suggest
relevant credentials that

are saved by the
password manager

and give the user an
option to autofill them.

Our doggy also wants to
toggle the visibility
of their autofilled password.
Let's add that in.

We'll update the
functionality of the icon

in the trailing section
of the password text field

to not only change
icon while clicked,

but also toggle the
password's visibility.

To do this, we'll use
textObfuscationMode.

textObfuscationMode
is a new feature

which allows you to define
the visibility of the text
in a SecureTextField.
There are three options--

Visible, which shows
anything typed;

Hidden, which hides
all character input;

and RevealLastTyped
which is a new API that

shows the last character
typed for a set amount of time
before hiding it.

Our code has a variable called
passwordVisible that is updated
when the icon is clicked.

If passwordVisible is true, we
will set textObfuscationMode

to Visible, and if not, we'll
set it as RevealLastTyped.
Voila!

We can now toggle our
password's visibility.

After logging in, we will want
to add an extra protection

to make sure no one
steals our darling doggo's
personal information.

I'll pass it off
to Halil to explain
two-factor authentication.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
HALIL OZERCAN: Thank you, Megan.

Let's talk about how we can make
this two-factor authentication

experience both
exceptional and delightful.

First, we want to have a more
customized look and feel.

Just a regular TextField
is kind of boring.

And second, we want
to use Autofill system

to quickly fill this field
from the received message.

We start with our
first goal, the design.

Right now, our app shows
a regular TextField
like on the left.

Instead, we would like
this bespoke look and feel

on the right, a field
that is specifically
designed to accept an OTP code.
So how do we do it?

Intuitively, we
may think that we

can achieve this look with
six separate TextFields placed
in a row.

But then we will need to
guarantee that each TextField
only accepts a single digit.

Also, after a digit
is typed, the focus

should switch to the
next field and also

back to the previous
field when erased.

And eventually, when
Submit is clicked,

we will have to concatenate the
contents of all six TextField

states to have
the full OTP code.

This is already so much work,
and there is more to address.

But there's a way we can
keep using a single TextField
and achieve the desired design.

Here, we have the current code
with this TextField setup.

It limits the length
to six characters,

configures the keyboard
to show numbers,

and also disables
multiline entries.

So it is set up
correctly and works fine.

It just lacks that custom
look that can make it special.

Let's start with simply
switching our TextField
to BasicTextField.

BasicTextField is the
underlying composable

from foundation module that
all TextFields depend on.

It can be used to build
more stylistic TextFields

appropriate for
any design system.

After switching to
the basic TextField,

our OTP field looks like this
because BasicTextField does not

offer any styling or
decorations whatsoever.

But this is OK because we want
an entirely different layout
anyway.

The difference we
just saw mainly

comes from how material text
field uses the decorator
parameter of BasicTextField.

Material TextFields use it to
add labels, icons, paddings,

background color, and any
other visual decoration
around innerTextField.

Here, the red-bordered regions
show where innerTextField
is exactly located.
The rest is the decorator.

We are also going to use
the decorator parameter
to implement our unique design.

However, we will skip calling
this innerTextField parameter

since we want to
replace the inner text
rendering with six-digit boxes.

But be careful--
this may not always
be the best option for you.

InnerTextField is responsible
for a lot of things,

like rendering the text
itself, enabling gestures

like tap or long click, showing
selection, and cursor handles.

Luckily, we don't need most
of those for our OTP field.

Maybe, except for showing the
text toolbar, to paste text
from the clipboard.

Here, we recommend letting the
keyboard handle this problem.

You can see here
that Gboard suggests
pasting the copied text.

Users can paste
through the keyboard
without needing the toolbar.

Or, shortly, we will
talk about Autofill,

which makes even copy-pasting
look like so much work.

Let's continue from
where we left off.

We are skipping innerTextField
and writing our own decoration
with a unique layout.

We know that we
are going to have

a row of six-digit composables
as decoration for our TextField.

Here is the reference
implementation
for the digit composable.

There is nothing much
interesting about it.

It just shows the number
in a nice-looking box.

Each digit composable
is going to get

its corresponding character
from the TextField state
according to its index.

For missing characters, we
just pass an empty space
to the digit composable.
Well, that's just really it.

No need to manage
multiple TextFields.

A single basic text field
with a custom decorator
achieves our design goals.

BasicTextField still has
its core functionality.

We only replace the part that
shows the TextField's text
content with our own layout.

We have completed
our unique layout.

Now, let's add a way
to automatically fill
in the two-factor code.

We can do this through
Autofill integration.

In the login
screen, Autofill was

used to fill the information
that the user had already saved
into their password manager.

For two-factor
authentication, we

can use Autofill to fill the
OTP code from a text message.

Not surprisingly,
we are again going

to use the semantics modifier
and ContentType parameter.

This time, the ContentType
needs to be SMS OTP code.

The OTP code message arrives
after the user successfully
logs in.

The system asks whether we want
to use the Autofill service.

After we say yes, the code
is entered automatically
from the new message.
And that's it.

We completed the
planned improvements

for our two-factor
authentication screen.

Now that the pup can get into
the app, let's talk about one

of the most important parts
of a social media app, chat.

Currently, on this screen, our
pup can text with his pack,

but sometimes woofs
and barks are not

enough to express
his many emotions,

so we will give him a way
to use GIFs or stickers
to be more expressive.

But he is also an Android
wizard with a foldable.

He likes to
multitask, so he wants

to share his favorite
photos via drag and drop.

We asked our pup what ways he
likes to send his adorable GIFs.

He says that he usually
uses GIFs from the keyboard,

or sometimes he copies
and pastes cute images
from his favorite website.

And of course, the one he
likes the most, drag and drop
from other apps with his paws.

Unfortunately,
TextField composables

are not implicitly
configured to handle

this type of content coming
from any of these sources.

This is because TextField
is just a text component.

Receiving rich content
requires extra logic and care.

Therefore, we introduced
the contentReceiver modifier

to unify all these different
methods into a single API

so that you can do less but
gain more on user experience.

This API is currently
marked as experimental,

so it is subject to possible
API changes in the future,

but its implementation is
considered stable and ready
for use.

You can start by simply
adding contentReceiver
to your modifier chain.

Having this modifier is
enough to let the keyboard

know that this TextField
now supports GIFs, stickers,

and any other rich content,
but we have more to do.

The contentReceiver
modifier takes

a lambda that receives a
transferableContent object.

TransferableContent is mainly
a wrapper around clip data
and clip description APIs.

You can learn more about how
to handle this type of data
in the linked documentation.

We will process this
transferableContent
in our ViewModel.

Here inside our ViewModel,
we keep track of two states.

One is a regular TextField
state for our message field.

The other one is
a list of images

to be sent with
the next message.

Then, inside our
handleContent function,

we iterate the received items
inside transferableContent

and extract URI addresses using
the provided Consume function.

We are going to assume each
URI points to an image file.

We update our selected images
list with these new URIs.

These files may require
further analysis

to check whether the actual
content, image content.

We leave this out as
a further exercise.
There we have it.

Our pup can use
his favorite GIFs

to tell others what
a good boy he is.

But contentReceiver has one
more trick up its sleeve.

And you can tell that there
might be more to this modifier

by realizing that
contentReceiver is not
tied to TextField at all.
It is just a modifier.
We can apply it anywhere.

We can put it on a parent
composable of our TextField,
and it would still work.

This is because TextField looks
for this modifier on its parent

chain, not just in
its own modifier.

But then what would
be the use of placing

this modifier anywhere
else than our TextField?
The answer is drag and drop.

The composable that has
contentReceiver attached to it
becomes the actual drop zone.

When we drag and apply
the modifier on TextField,

this would be the
only place where
we can drop the dragging items.

However, when contentReceiver
is applied on the entire screen,

everywhere becomes a drop zone,
makes it easier to target.
How about that?

We add the drag and
drop integration

without doing
anything additional.

We only move the contentReceiver
modifier to the screen level.

And with that, our chat
experience is perfect.

Also, I hear that Meghan
is working on something
special for our pup.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
MEGHAN MEHTA: Thank you, Halil.

This social media app
also provides the option

to buy a customized collar
for our fluffy bestie.

However, the text sizing looks
off on the front of the collar,

and the phone number
does not format properly.

In this section, we
will size the name

on the collar based
on length and format

the phone number in
the American format.

To fix the name sizing issue,
we can use the AutoSize API,

which allows the text to take up
as much space as possible given
the parent size constraints.

To the composable that
displays the text,

we will add the autoSize
parameter and set it
to TextAutoSize.StepBased().

This allows us to
take up as much space

as makes sense, based on
how much text there is
and how much space there is.

The best part is that it
just works out of the box.

The next most important
part of a caller

is to have a phone
number, in case
our fluffy friend goes missing.

Currently, the number
isn't formatted at all

and just looks like
a jumble of digits.

We can use TextField's new
input and output transformations
to properly format this number.

In this example, we will
be formatting this number
like an American phone number.

We want to add the parentheses
and the dash through formatting

and not have our
puppy type them in.

Here's the flow of how input and
output transformations happen.

Currently, we're
receiving input.

Input can come from
multiple places, including

but not limited to keyboard,
paste, and selection.

Here are some examples of
inputs that may come in.

The input will go through
an inputTransformation,

which you can think of
as a filter function.

In our case, we want
to limit the length

to 10 characters, which is how
long an American number is,

and we only want
digits to be passed in.

Let's start by limiting
the length of what
can be input to 10 characters.

Then we can chain
InputTransformations

and add a restriction for
the input to only be digits.

Now the text is
limited to 10 digits,

and the text with the
InputTransformation
is saved in the TextField state.

The number still
needs to be formatted,

so we'll add an
OutputTransformation.

In order to format an American
phone number properly,

the opening parentheses
is at index 0,

the closing parentheses
is at index 4,
and the dash is at index 8.

For this
OutputTransformation, we

will be using a
TextFieldBuffer, which
is similar to a StringBuilder.

This will allow
you to change text

through methods like Insert,
Replace, Delete, and Append.

We'll call transformOutput()
on a TextFieldBuffer and apply

the formatting rules we defined
for American phone numbers.

We can add that
outputTransformation

to the TextField's
OutputTransformation parameter.

Now the text is outputting a
properly formatted phone number.

Here's a close up of
our full transformation,

including both input and
output transformations.

And here it is in action
on the caller screen.

We can see that it's also
taking advantage of AutoSize.
This color looks paw-some.

In this talk we covered
APIs like Autofill,

state-based TextField,
AutoSize, and contentReceiver

that can make your app more
functional, exceptional,
and delightful.

We hope that you've
learned something new
and had some fun along the way.

Thank you so much
for joining us.

I'm Meghan, and I
was joined by Halil.
Happy coding.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
